rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF MUI TAI VONG
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion, Motion And Memorandum Of Points Of Authorities For Motion To Stay Probate Proceedings Of The Estate Of Mui Tai Vong
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. Decedent Mui Tai Vong died on March 22, 2020. She left behind two adult children: a daughter, Nam Muoi Au, and a son, Sang Au Su. The instant Estate proceeding goes back to June 2021, when Phat Vinh Cam and Au filed a Petition for Probate and for Letters with Will Annexed, seeking their own appointment to administer decedent's estate under a July 2010 will. On July 14, 2021, Su filed an opposition to Cam and Au's Petition for Probate. On August 19, 2022, Cam and Au filed a Petition for Order Confirming Title to Real Property ("Cam and Au's Section 850 Petition"), seeking an order that the 1/3 interest in the real property known as 919 Rutland Street in San Francisco, California, which is titled in the decedent, should, in fact, be titled to Cam and Au. The next day, Su filed a competing Petition for Probate seeking his own appointment to administer Decedent's estate, also under the July 2010 will. The issue of ownership of 919 Rutland Street, at least as among Decedent's estate, Cam and Au and Su and his family, was joined in this Estate proceeding by mid-2021. In October 2021, December 2021 and March 2022, the court granted the parties' requests for continuances, the latter requests based on a stipulation to mediate the disputes. After it was clear mediation was not imminent, the court set trial on Cam and Au's Section 850 Petition for May 31, 2023. In the meantime, on July 6, 2022, Su, Hing Yip Su and Mimi Su ("the Sus") initiated a civil action, Su, et al v. Au, et al. (CGC-22-600632), by filing their Verified Complaint for: 1. Quiet Title; 2. Fraud; 3. Negligent Misrepresentation; 4.Civil Conspiracy; 5. Negligence; 6. Reformation; 7. Declaratory Relief. The central allegation in the civil complaint, is that the current title on 919 Rutland Street was fraudulently obtained by Au and Cam, and it wrongly removed Su from title as a joint tenant, thus the 1/3 interest in the real property known as 919 Rutland Street in San Francisco, California, which is titled in the decedent, should, in fact, be titled to Su. The Sus filed the civil action "for the purpose of determining the true ownership interests in the Property for the benefit of the Estate." (Motion at 7:15-19.) The Sus now seek an order staying these Estate proceedings in favor of proceedings in the new civil action. The motion is DENIED. This motion is brought solely under Code of Civil Procedure section 128 (Notice of Motion at 2:12-14). The court declines to exercise its discretion in favor of a stay of these proceedings. The court is not persuaded that the Estate proceeding, which was initiated more than a year before the civil action and in which a trial is currently set, should not proceed. [Part 1 of 3]

rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 3] Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on September 15, 2022. Remote appearance is available consistent with San Francisco Superior Court Local Rules, rule 14.1(D). To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-1-669-254-5252 and key in meeting ID "160 225 4765#", then participant ID "0#", then password "514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. [Part 3 of 3]

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF MUI TAI VONG
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion, Motion And Memorandum Of Points Of Authorities For Motion To Stay Probate Proceedings Of The Estate Of Mui Tai Vong (Added For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] This probate department is a court of general jurisdiction, with the same power and authority with respect to proceedings as otherwise provided by law to a superior court or judge of the superior court under Code of Civil Procedure section 128. (Prob. C., section 800.) As a court of general jurisdiction, this department can hear causes of action or matters normally raised in civil actions to the extent such matters are factually related to a probate petition, including competing claims over title to and distribution of decedent's property. (See Prob. C., section 855; see also Estate of Kraus (2010) 184 CA4th 103, 111, 114.) Here, nothing in this record suggests that the Sus' civil claims cannot not be fully adjudicated in the Estate proceeding, for example, by way of a competing Section 850 petition under Probate Code section 850(a)(2)(c). To the extent a claim cannot be adjudicated in a probate proceeding, the court can order that it be determined in a separate civil action. (See (Prob. C., section 801.) The Sus breathlessly contend that to deny their motion would be to deny "their day in court." (Motion at 11:8-12:4.) Given the above facts, their contention is without basis. In any event, they have failed to identify a single way in which allowing the ongoing Estate proceeding to continue would bar them from "defending their rights" or frustrate their effort to "determine the true ownership of the Property." (See Motion at 12:2-3.) Additionally, the Sus seem to argue that staying this action in favor of the civil action will allow the parties to resolve title to 919 Rutland more expeditiously. (Motion at 12:14-22.). The argument misses the mark. The Section 850 Petition is set for trial in May 2023, whereas the civil action has just commenced and has no trial date. There can be no doubt that the Estate proceeding is further along. Moreover, by the motion, the Sus seek an order staying the entire Estate proceeding. The Sus have not shown that proceedings on either of the competing Petitions for Letters of Administration should be stayed in favor of the civil action. Indeed, the court has a hard time seeing how the civil action could proceed absent a court-appointed Estate representative. Insofar as it seeks to strip title from Decedent's Estate, an Estate representative will have to be a party. If the Estate proceeding were stayed now, the Estate would be without a representative and the civil action would be without a key party. Cam and Au argue that the Sus' motion is frivolous, and they ask the court to impose sanction of $3,357. Presumably, Cam and Au move under Code of Civil Procedure section 128.5. They have not shown that they complied with the statutory safe harbor provision. (See Code Civ. Proc., section 128.5, subd. (f)(1)(B).) The request, therefore, must be denied. [Part 2 of 3]
